units.title: Of The Holy Scriptures
content: 1.1 The Holy Scripture is the only sufficient, certain, and infallible rule of all saving knowledge, faith, and obedience. {{proofs: 2Ti 3:15-17; Isa 8:20; Luk 16:29, 31; Eph 2:20}} Although the light of nature, and the works of creation and providence do so far manifest the goodness, wisdom, and power of God, as to leave men inexcusable; yet they are not sufficient to give that knowledge of God and His will which is necessary unto salvation. {{proofs: Rom 1:19-21; 2:14-15; Psa 19:1-3}} Therefore it pleased the Lord at sundry times and in divers manners to reveal Himself, and to declare that His will unto His church; {{proofs: Heb 1:1}} and afterward for the better preserving and propagating of the truth, and for the more sure establishment and comfort of the church against the corruption of the flesh, and the malice of Satan, and of the world, to commit the same wholly unto writing; which maketh the Holy Scriptures to be most necessary, those former ways of God's revealing His will unto His people being now ceased. {{proofs: Pro 22:19-21; Rom 15:4; 2Pe 1:19-20}}

1.2 Under the name of Holy Scripture, or the Word of God written, are now contained all the books of the Old and New Testaments, which are these: Of the Old Testament: Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy, Joshua, Judges, Ruth, 1 Samuel, 2 Samuel, 1 Kings, 2 Kings, 1 Chronicles, 2 Chronicles, Ezra, Nehemiah, Ester, Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, The Song of Solomon, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, Daniel, Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi. Of the New Testament: Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, Acts, Romans, 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 Thessalonians, 2 Thessalonians, 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, Philemon, Hebrews, James, 1 Peter, 2 Peter, 1 John, 2 John, 3 John, Jude, Revelation. All of which are given by the inspiration of God, to be the rule of faith and life. {{proofs: 2Ti 3:16}}

1.3 The books commonly called Apocrypha, not being of divine inspiration, are no part of the canon or rule of the Scripture, and, therefore, are of no authority to the church of God, nor to be any otherwise approved or made use of than other human writings. {{proofs: Luk 24:27, 44; Rom 3:2}}

1.4 The authority of the Holy Scripture, for which it ought to be believed, dependeth not upon the testimony of any man or church, but wholly upon God (who is truth itself), the author thereof; therefore it is to be received because it is the Word of God. {{proofs: 2Pe 1:19-21; 2Ti 3:16; 2Th 2:13; 1Jn 5:9}}

1.5 We may be moved and induced by the testimony of the church of God to a high and reverent esteem of the Holy Scriptures; and the heavenliness of the matter, the efficacy of the doctrine, and the majesty of the style, the consent of all the parts, the scope of the whole (which is to give all glory to God), the full discovery it makes of the only way of man's salvation, and many other incomparable excellencies, and entire perfections thereof, are arguments whereby it doth abundantly evidence itself to be the Word of God; yet notwithstanding, our full persuasion and assurance of the infallible truth, and divine authority thereof, is from the inward work of the Holy Spirit bearing witness by and with the Word in our hearts. {{proofs: Jhn 16:13-14; 1Co 2:10-12; 1Jn 2:20, 27}}

1.6 The whole counsel of God concerning all things necessary for His own glory, man's salvation, faith and life, is either expressly set down or necessarily contained in the Holy Scripture: unto which nothing at any time is to be added, whether by new revelation of the Spirit, or traditions of men. {{proofs: 2Ti 3:15-17; Gal 1:8-9}} Nevertheless, we acknowledge the inward illumination of the Spirit of God to be necessary for the saving understanding of such things as are revealed in the Word, {{proofs: Jhn 6:45; 1Co 2:9-12}} and that there are some circumstances concerning the worship of God, and government of the church, common to human actions and societies, which are to be ordered by the light of nature and Christian prudence, according to the general rules of the Word, which are always to be observed. {{proofs: 1Co 11:13-14; 14:26, 40}}

1.7 All things in Scripture are not alike plain in themselves, nor alike clear unto all; {{proofs: 2Pe 3:16}} yet those things which are necessary to be known, believed and observed for salvation, are so clearly propounded and opened in some place of Scripture or other, that not only the learned, but the unlearned, in a due use of ordinary means, may attain to a sufficient understanding of them. {{proofs: Psa 19:7; 119:130}}

1.8 The Old Testament in Hebrew (which was the native language of the people of God of old), {{proofs: Rom 3:2}} and the New Testament in Greek (which at the time of the writing of it was most generally known to the nations), being immediately inspired by God, and by His singular care and providence kept pure in all ages, are therefore authentic; so as in all controversies of religion, the church is finally to appeal to them. {{proofs: Isa 8:20}} But because these original tongues are not known to all the people of God, who have a right unto, and interest in the Scriptures, and are commanded in the fear of God to read, {{proofs: Act 15:15}} and search them, {{proofs: Jhn 5:39}} therefore they are to be translated into the vulgar language of every nation unto which they come, {{proofs: 1Co 14:6, 9, 11-12, 24, 28}} that the Word of God dwelling plentifully in all, they may worship Him in an acceptable manner, and through patience and comfort of the Scriptures may have hope. {{proofs: Col 3:16}}

1.9 The infallible rule of interpretation of Scripture is the Scripture itself; and therefore when there is a question about the true and full sense of any Scripture (which is not manifold, but one), it must be searched by other places that speak more clearly. {{proofs: 2Pe 1:20-21; Act 15:15-16}}

1.10 The supreme judge, by which all controversies of religion are to be determined, and all decrees of councils, opinions of ancient writers, doctrines of men, and private spirits, are to be examined, and in whose sentence we are to rest, can be no other but the Holy Scripture delivered by the Spirit, into which Scripture so delivered, our faith is finally resolved. {{proofs: Mat 22:29, 31-32; Eph 2:20; Act 28:23}}

units.title: Of God And Of The Holy Trinity
content: 2.1 The Lord our God is but one only living and true God; {{proofs: 1Co 8:4, 6; Deu 6:4}} whose subsistence is in and of Himself, {{proofs: Jer 10:10; Isa 48:12}} infinite in being and perfection; whose essence cannot be comprehended by any but Himself; {{proofs: Exo 3:14}} a most pure spirit, {{proofs: Jhn 4:24}} invisible, without body, parts, or passions, who only hath immortality, dwelling in the light which no man can approach unto; {{proofs: 1Ti 1:17; Deu 4:15-16}} who is immutable, {{proofs: Mal 3:6}} immense, {{proofs: 1Ki 8:27; Jer 23:23}} eternal, {{proofs: Psa 90:2}} incomprehensible, almighty, {{proofs: Gen 17:1}} every way infinite, most holy, {{proofs: Isa 6:3}} most wise, most free, most absolute; working all things according to the counsel of His own immutable and most righteous will, {{proofs: Psa 115:3; Isa 46:10}} for His own glory; {{proofs: Pro 16:4; Rom 11:36}} most loving, gracious, merciful, long-suffering, abundant in goodness and truth, forgiving iniquity, transgression, and sin; the rewarder of them that diligently seek Him, {{proofs: Exo 34:6-7; Heb 11:6}} and withal most just and terrible in His judgments, {{proofs: Neh 9:32-33}} hating all sin, {{proofs: Psa 5:5-6}} and who will by no means clear the guilty. {{proofs: Exo 34:7; Nah 1:2-3}}

2.2 God, having all life, {{proofs: Jhn 5:26}} glory, {{proofs: Psa 148:13}} goodness, {{proofs: Psa 119:68}} blessedness, in and of Himself, is alone in and unto Himself all-sufficient, not standing in need of any creature which He hath made, nor deriving any glory from them, {{proofs: Job 22:2-3}} but only manifesting His own glory in, by, unto, and upon them; He is the alone fountain of all being, of whom, through whom, and to whom are all things, {{proofs: Rom 11:34-36}} and He hath most sovereign dominion over all creatures, to do by them, for them, or upon them, whatsoever Himself pleaseth; {{proofs: Dan 4:25, 34-35}} in His sight all things are open and manifest, {{proofs: Heb 4:13}} His knowledge is infinite, infallible, and independent upon the creature, so as nothing is to Him contingent or uncertain; {{proofs: Ezk 11:5; Act 15:18}} He is most holy in all His counsels, in all His works, {{proofs: Psa 145:17}} and in all His commands; to Him is due from angels and men, whatsoever worship, {{proofs: Rev 5:12-14}} service, or obedience, as creatures they owe unto the Creator, and whatever He is further pleased to require of them.

2.3 In this divine and infinite Being there are three subsistences, the Father, the Word (or Son), and Holy Spirit, {{proofs: 1Jn 5:7; Mat 28:19; 2Co 13:14}} of one substance, power, and eternity, each having the whole divine essence, yet the essence undivided: {{proofs: Exo 3:14; Jhn 14:11; 1Co 8:6}} the Father is of none, neither begotten nor proceeding; the Son is eternally begotten of the Father; {{proofs: Jhn 1:14, 18}} the Holy Spirit proceeding from the Father and the Son; {{proofs: Jhn 15:26; Gal 4:6}} all infinite, without beginning, therefore but one God, who is not to be divided in nature and being, but distinguished by several peculiar relative properties and personal relations; which doctrine of the Trinity is the foundation of all our communion with God, and comfortable dependence on Him.

units.title: Of God's Decree
content: 3.1 God hath decreed in himself, from all eternity, by the most wise and holy counsel of His own will, freely and unchangeably, all things, whatsoever comes to pass; {{proofs: Isa 46:10; Eph 1:11; Heb 6:17; Rom 9:15, 18}} yet so as thereby is God neither the author of sin nor hath fellowship with any therein; {{proofs: Jas 1:13; 1Jn 1:5}} nor is violence offered to the will of the creature, nor yet is the liberty or contingency of second causes taken away, but rather established; {{proofs: Act 4:27-28; Jhn 19:11}} in which appears His wisdom in disposing all things, and power and faithfulness in accomplishing His decree. {{proofs: Num 23:19; Eph 1:3-5}}

3.2 Although God knoweth whatsoever may or can come to pass, upon all supposed conditions, {{proofs: Act 15:18}} yet hath He not decreed anything, because He foresaw it as future, or as that which would come to pass upon such conditions. {{proofs: Rom 9:11, 13, 16, 18}}

3.3 By the decree of God, for the manifestation of His glory, some men and angels are predestinated, or foreordained to eternal life through Jesus Christ, {{proofs: 1Ti 5:21; Mat 25:34}} to the praise of His glorious grace; {{proofs: Eph 1:5-6}} others being left to act in their sin to their just condemnation, to the praise of His glorious justice. {{proofs: Rom 9:22-23; Jud 4}}

3.4 These angels and men thus predestinated and foreordained, are particularly and unchangeably designed, and their number so certain and definite, that it cannot be either increased or diminished. {{proofs: 2Ti 2:19; Jhn 13:18}}

3.5 Those of mankind that are predestinated to life, God, before the foundation of the world was laid, according to His eternal and immutable purpose, and the secret counsel and good pleasure of His will, hath chosen in Christ unto everlasting glory, out of His mere free grace and love, {{proofs: Eph 1:4, 9, 11; Rom 8:30; 2Ti 1:9; 1Th 5:9}} without any other thing in the creature as a condition or cause moving Him thereunto. {{proofs: Rom 9:13, 16; Eph 2:5, 12}}

3.6 As God hath appointed the elect unto glory, so He hath, by the eternal and most free purpose of His will, foreordained all the means thereunto; {{proofs: 1Pe 1:2, 2}} wherefore they who are elected, being fallen in Adam, are redeemed by Christ, {{proofs: 1Th 5:9-10}} are effectually called unto faith in Christ, by His Spirit working in due season, are justified, adopted, sanctified, {{proofs: Rom 8:30; 2Th 2:13}} and kept by His power through faith unto salvation; {{proofs: 1Pe 1:5}} neither are any other redeemed by Christ, or effectually called, justified, adopted, sanctified, and saved, but the elect only. {{proofs: Jhn 10:26; 17:9; 6:64}}

3.7 The doctrine of this high mystery of predestination is to be handled with special prudence and care, that men attending the will of God revealed in His Word, and yielding obedience thereunto, may, from the certainty of their effectual vocation, be assured of their eternal election; {{proofs: 1Th 1:4-5; 2Pe 1:10}} so shall this doctrine afford matter of praise, {{proofs: Eph 1:6; Rom 11:33}} reverence, and admiration of God, and of humility, {{proofs: Rom 11:5-6, 20}} diligence, and abundant consolation to all that sincerely obey the gospel. {{proofs: Luk 10:20}}

units.title: Of Creation
content: 4.1 In the beginning it pleased God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, {{proofs: Jhn 1:2-3; Heb 1:2; Job 26:13}} for the manifestation of the glory of His eternal power, {{proofs: Rom 1:20}} wisdom, and goodness, to create or make the world, and all things therein, whether visible or invisible, in the space of six days, and all very good. {{proofs: Col 1:16; Gen 1:31}}

4.2 After God had made all other creatures, He created man, male and female, {{proofs: Gen 1:27}} with reasonable and immortal souls, {{proofs: Gen 2:7}} rendering them fit unto that life to God for which they were created; being made after the image of God, in knowledge, righteousness, and true holiness; {{proofs: Ecc 7:29; Gen 1:26}} having the law of God written in their hearts, {{proofs: Rom 2:14-15}} and power to fulfill it, and yet under a possibility of transgressing, being left to the liberty of their own will, which was subject to change. {{proofs: Gen 3:6}}

4.3 Besides the law written in their hearts, they received a command not to eat of the tree of knowledge of good and evil, {{proofs: Gen 2:17}} which while they kept, they were happy in their communion with God, and had dominion over the creatures. {{proofs: Gen 1:26, 28}}

units.title: Of Divine Providence
content: 5.1 God the good Creator of all things, in His infinite power and wisdom does uphold, direct, dispose, and govern all creatures and things, {{proofs: Heb 1:3; Job 38:11; Isa 46:10-11; Psa 135:6}} from the greatest even to the least, {{proofs: Mat 10:29-31}} by His most wise and holy providence, to the end for the which they were created, according unto His infallible foreknowledge, and the free and immutable counsel of His own will; to the praise of the glory of His wisdom, power, justice, infinite goodness, and mercy. {{proofs: Eph 1}}

5.2 Although in relation to the foreknowledge and decree of God, the first cause, all things come to pass immutably and infallibly; {{proofs: Act 2:23}} so that there is not anything befalls any by chance, or without His providence; {{proofs: Pro 16:33}} yet by the same providence He ordereth them to fall out according to the nature of second causes, either necessarily, freely, or contingently. {{proofs: Gen 8:22}}

5.3 God, in his ordinary providence maketh use of means, {{proofs: Act 27:31, 44; Isa 55:10-11}} yet is free to work without, {{proofs: Hos 1:7}} above, {{proofs: Rom 4:19-21}} and against them {{proofs: Dan 3:27}} at His pleasure.

5.4 The almighty power, unsearchable wisdom, and infinite goodness of God, so far manifest themselves in His providence, that His determinate counsel extends itself even to the first fall, and all other sinful actions both of angels and men; {{proofs: Rom 11:32-34; 2Sa 24:1; 1Ch 21:1}} and that not by a bare permission, which also He most wisely and powerfully boundeth, and otherwise ordereth and governeth, {{proofs: 2Ki 19:28; Psa 76:10}} in a manifold dispensation to His most holy ends; {{proofs: Gen 1:20; Isa 10:6-7, 12}} yet so, as the sinfulness of their acts proceedeth only from the creatures, and not from God, who, being most holy and righteous, neither is nor can be the author or approver of sin. {{proofs: Psa 1; 21; 1Jn 2:16}}

5.5 The most wise, righteous, and gracious God doth often times leave for a season His own children to manifold temptations and the corruptions of their own hearts, to chastise them for their former sins, or to discover unto them the hidden strength of corruption and deceitfulness of their hearts, that they may be humbled; and to raise them to a more close and constant dependence for their support upon Himself; and to make them more watchful against all future occasions of sin, and for other just and holy ends. {{proofs: 2Ch 32:25-26, 31; 2Co 12:7-9}} So that whatsoever befalls any of His elect is by His appointment, for His glory, and their good. {{proofs: Rom 8:28}}

5.6 As for those wicked and ungodly men whom God, as the righteous judge, for former sin does blind and harden; {{proofs: Rom 1; 11:7-8}} from them He not only withholdeth His grace, whereby they might have been enlightened in their understanding, and wrought upon their hearts; {{proofs: Deu 29:4}} but sometimes also withdraweth the gifts which they had, {{proofs: Mat 13:12}} and exposeth them to such objects as their corruption makes occasion of sin; {{proofs: Deu 2:30; 2Ki 8:12-13}} and withal, gives them over to their own lusts, the temptations of the world, and the power of Satan, {{proofs: Psa 81:11-12; 2Th 2:10-12}} whereby it comes to pass that they harden themselves, under those means which God useth for the softening of others. {{proofs: Exo 8:15, 32; Isa 6:9-10; 1Pe 2:7-8}}

5.7 As the providence of God doth in general reach to all creatures, so after a more special manner it taketh care of His church, and disposeth of all things to the good thereof. {{proofs: 1Ti 4:10; Amo 9:8-9; Isa 43:3-5}}

units.title: Of The Fall Of Man, Of Sin, And Of The Punishment Thereof
content: 6.1 Although God created man upright and perfect, and gave him a righteous law, which had been unto life had he kept it, and threatened death upon the breach thereof, {{proofs: Gen 2:16-17}} yet he did not long abide in this honor; Satan using the subtlety of the serpent to seduce Eve, then by her seducing Adam, who, without any compulsion, did willfully transgress the law of their creation, and the command given unto them, in eating the forbidden fruit, {{proofs: Gen 3:12-13; 2Co 11:3}} which God was pleased, according to His wise and holy counsel to permit, having purposed to order it to His own glory.

6.2 Our first parents, by this sin, fell from their original righteousness and communion with God, and we in them whereby death came upon all: {{proofs: Rom 3:23}} all becoming dead in sin, {{proofs: Rom 5:12}} and wholly defiled in all the faculties and parts of soul and body. {{proofs: Tit 1:15; Gen 6:5; Jer 17:9; Rom 3:10-19}}

6.3 They being the root, and by God's appointment, standing in the room and stead of all mankind, the guilt of the sin was imputed, and corrupted nature conveyed, to all their posterity descending from them by ordinary generation, {{proofs: Rom 5:12-19; 1Co 15:21-22, 45, 49}} being now conceived in sin, {{proofs: Psa 51:5; Job 14:4}} and by nature children of wrath, {{proofs: Eph 2:3}} the servants of sin, the subjects of death, {{proofs: Rom 6:20; 5:12}} and all other miseries, spiritual, temporal, and eternal, unless the Lord Jesus set them free. {{proofs: Heb 2:14-15; 1Th 1:10}}

6.4 From this original corruption, whereby we are utterly indisposed, disabled, and made opposite to all good, and wholly inclined to all evil, {{proofs: Rom 8:7; Col 1:21}} do proceed all actual transgressions. {{proofs: Jas 1:14-15; Mat 15:19}}

6.5 The corruption of nature, during this life, does remain in those that are regenerated; {{proofs: Rom 7:18, 23; Ecc 7:20; 1Jn 1:8}} and although it be through Christ pardoned and mortified, yet both itself, and the first motions thereof, are truly and properly sin. {{proofs: Rom 7:23-25; Gal 5:17}}

units.title: Of God's Covenant
content: 7.1 The distance between God and the creature is so great, that although reasonable creatures do owe obedience to Him as their creator, yet they could never have attained the reward of life but by some voluntary condescension on God's part, which He hath been pleased to express by way of covenant. {{proofs: Luk 17:10; Job 35:7-8}}

7.2 Moreover, man having brought himself under the curse of the law by his fall, it pleased the Lord to make a covenant of grace, {{proofs: Gen 2:17; Gal 3:10; Rom 3:20-21}} wherein He freely offers unto sinners life and salvation by Jesus Christ, requiring of them faith in Him, that they may be saved; {{proofs: Rom 8:3; Mrk 16:15-16; Jhn 3:16}} and promising to give unto all those that are ordained unto eternal life, His Holy Spirit, to make them willing and able to believe. {{proofs: Ezk 36:26-27; Jhn 6:44-45; Psa 110:3}}

7.3 This covenant is revealed in the gospel; first of all to Adam in the promise of salvation by the seed of the woman, {{proofs: Gen 3:15}} and afterwards by farther steps, until the full discovery thereof was completed in the New Testament; {{proofs: Heb 1:1}} and it is founded in that eternal covenant transaction that was between the Father and the Son about the redemption of the elect; {{proofs: 2Ti 1:9; Tit 1:2}} and it is alone by the grace of this covenant that all the posterity of fallen Adam that ever were saved did obtain life and blessed immortality, man being now utterly incapable of acceptance with God upon those terms on which Adam stood in his state of innocency. {{proofs: Heb 11; 6; 13; Rom 4:1-2; Act 4:12; Jhn 8:56}}

units.title: Of Christ The Mediator
content: 8.1 It pleased God, in His eternal purpose, to choose and ordain the Lord Jesus, His only begotten Son, according to the covenant made between them both, to be the mediator between God and man; {{proofs: Isa 42:1; 1Pe 1:19-20}} the prophet, {{proofs: Act 3:22}} priest, {{proofs: Heb 5:5-6}} and king; {{proofs: Psa 2:6; Luk 1:33}} Head and Savior of His church, {{proofs: Eph 1:22-23}} the heir of all things, {{proofs: Heb 1:2}} and judge of the world; {{proofs: Act 17:31}} unto whom He did from all eternity give a people to be His seed and to be by Him in time redeemed, called, justified, sanctified, and glorified. {{proofs: Isa 53:10; Jhn 17:6; Rom 8:30}}

8.2 The Son of God, the second person in the Holy Trinity, being very and eternal God, the brightness of the Father's glory, of one substance and equal with Him, who made the world, who upholds and governs all things He has made, did, when the fullness of time was come, take upon Him man's nature, with all the essential properties and common infirmities of thereof, {{proofs: Jhn 1:14; Gal 4:4}} yet without sin; {{proofs: Rom 8:3; Heb 2:14, 16-17; 4:15}} being conceived by the Holy Spirit in the womb of the Virgin Mary, the Holy Spirit coming down upon her: and the power of the Most High overshadowing her; and so was made of a woman, of the tribe of Judah, of the seed of Abraham and David, according to the Scriptures; {{proofs: Mat 1:22-23}} so that two whole, perfect, and distinct natures were inseparably joined together in one person, without conversion, composition, or confusion; which person is very God and very man, yet one Christ, the only mediator between God and man. {{proofs: Luk 1:27, 31, 35; Rom 9:5; 1Ti 2:5}}

8.3 The Lord Jesus, in His human nature thus united to the divine, in the person of the Son, was sanctified and anointed with the Holy Spirit above measure, {{proofs: Psa 45:7; Act 10:38; Jhn 3:34}} having in Him all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge; {{proofs: Col 2:3}} in whom it pleased the Father that all fullness should dwell, {{proofs: Col 1:19}} to the end that being holy, harmless, undefiled, {{proofs: Heb 7:26}} and full of grace and truth, {{proofs: Jhn 1:14}} He might be throughly furnished to execute the office of mediator and surety; {{proofs: Heb 7:22}} which office He took not upon himself, but was thereunto called by His Father; {{proofs: Heb 5:5}} who also put all power and judgement in His hand, and gave Him commandment to execute the same. {{proofs: Jhn 5:22, 27; Mat 28:18; Act 2}}

8.4 This office the Lord Jesus did most willingly undertake, {{proofs: Psa 40:7-8; Heb 10:5-10; Jhn 10:18}} which that He might discharge He was made under the law, {{proofs: Gal 4:4; Mat 3:15}} and did perfectly fulfill it, and underwent the punishment due to us, which we should have born and suffered, {{proofs: Gal 3:13; Isa 53:6; 1Pe 3:18}} being made sin and a curse for us; {{proofs: 2Co 5:21}} enduring most grievous sorrows in His soul, and most painful sufferings in His body; {{proofs: Mat 26:37-38; Luk 22:44; Mat 27:46}} was crucified, and died, and remained in the state of the dead, yet saw no corruption: {{proofs: Act 13:37}} on the third day He arose from the dead {{proofs: 1Co 15:3-4}} with the same body in which He suffered, {{proofs: Jhn 20:25, 27}} with which He also ascended into heaven, {{proofs: Mrk 16:19; Act 1:9-11}} and there sits at the right hand of His Father making intercession, {{proofs: Rom 8:34; Heb 9:24}} and shall return to judge men and angels at the end of the world. {{proofs: Act 10:42; Rom 14:9-10; Act 1:11; 2Pe 2:4}}

8.5 The Lord Jesus, by His perfect obedience and sacrifice of Himself, which He through the eternal Spirit once offered up to God, has fully satisfied the justice of God, {{proofs: Heb 9:14; 10:14; Rom 3:25-26}} procured reconciliation, and purchased an everlasting inheritance in the kingdom of heaven, for all those whom the Father has given unto Him. {{proofs: Jhn 17:2; Heb 9:15}}

8.6 Although the price of redemption was not actually paid by Christ until after His incarnation, yet the virtue, efficacy, and benefit thereof were communicated to the elect in all ages successively from the beginning of the world, in and by those promises, types, and sacrifices wherein He was revealed, and signified to be the seed of the woman which should bruise the serpent's head; {{proofs: 1Co 4:10; Heb 4:2; 1Pe 1:10-11}} and the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world, {{proofs: Rev 13:8}} being the same yesterday, and today and for ever. {{proofs: Heb 13:8}}

8.7 Christ, in the work of mediation, acts according to both natures, by each nature doing that which is proper to itself; yet by reason of the unity of the person, that which is proper to one nature is sometimes in Scripture, attributed to the person denominated by the other nature. {{proofs: Jhn 3:13; Act 20:28}}

8.8 To all those for whom Christ has obtained eternal redemption, He does certainly and effectually apply and communicate the same, making intercession for them; {{proofs: Jhn 6:37; 10:15-16; 17:9; Rom 5:10}} uniting them to Himself by His Spirit, revealing to them, in and by His Word, the mystery of salvation, persuading them to believe and obey, {{proofs: Jhn 17:6; Eph 1:9; 1Jn 5:20}} governing their hearts by His Word and Spirit, {{proofs: Rom 8:9, 14}} and overcoming all their enemies by His almighty power and wisdom, {{proofs: Psa 110:1; 1Co 15:25-26}} in such manner and ways as are most consonant to His wonderful and unsearchable dispensation; and all of free and absolute grace, without any condition foreseen in them to procure it. {{proofs: Jhn 3:8; Eph 1:8}}

8.9 This office of mediator between God and man is proper only to Christ, who is the prophet, priest, and king of the church of God; and may not be either in whole, or any part thereof, transferred from Him to any other. {{proofs: 1Ti 2:5}}

8.10 This number and order of offices is necessary; for in respect of our ignorance, we stand in need of His prophetical office; {{proofs: Jhn 1:18}} and in respect of our alienation from God, and imperfection of the best of our services, we need His priestly office to reconcile us and present us acceptable unto God; {{proofs: Col 1:21; Gal 5:17}} and in respect to our averseness and utter inability to return to God, and for our rescue and security from our spiritual adversaries, we need His kingly office to convince, subdue, draw, uphold, deliver, and preserve us to His heavenly kingdom. {{proofs: Jhn 16:8; Psa 110:3; Luk 1:74-75}}

units.title: Of Free Will
content: 9.1 God has endued the will of man with that natural liberty and power of acting upon choice, that it is neither forced, nor by any necessity of nature determined to do good or evil. {{proofs: Mat 17:12; Jas 1:14; Deu 30:19}}

9.2 Man, in his state of innocency, had freedom and power to will and to do that which was good and well-pleasing to God, {{proofs: Ecc 7:29}} but yet was mutable so that he might fall from it. {{proofs: Gen 3:6}}

9.3 Man, by his fall into a state of sin, has wholly lost all ability of will to any spiritual good accompanying salvation; {{proofs: Rom 5:6; 8:7}} so as a natural man, being altogether averse from that good, and dead in sin, {{proofs: Eph 2:1, 5}} is not able by his own strength to convert himself, or to prepare himself thereunto. {{proofs: Tit 3:3-5; Jhn 6:44}}

9.4 When God converts a sinner, and translates him into the state of grace, He frees him from his natural bondage under sin, {{proofs: Col 1:13; Jhn 8:36}} and by His grace alone enables him freely to will and to do that which is spiritually good; {{proofs: Php 2:13}} yet so as that by reason of his remaining corruptions, he does not perfectly, nor only will that which is good, but does also will that which is evil. {{proofs: Rom 7:15, 18-19, 21, 23}}
9.5 This will of man is made perfectly and immutably free to good alone in the state of glory only. {{proofs: Eph 4:13}}

units.title: Of Effectual Calling
content: 10.1 Those whom God hath predestinated unto life, He is pleased in His appointed and accepted time, effectually to call, {{proofs: Rom 8:30; 11:7; Eph 1:10-11; 2Th 2:13-14}} by His Word and Spirit, out of that state of sin and death in which they are by nature, to grace and salvation by Jesus Christ; {{proofs: Eph 2:1-6}} enlightening their minds spiritually and savingly to understand the things of God; {{proofs: Act 26:18; Eph 1:17-18}} taking away their heart of stone; and giving to them a heart of flesh; {{proofs: Ezk 36:26}} renewing their wills, and by His almighty power determining them to that which is good, and effectually drawing them to Jesus Christ; {{proofs: Deu 30:6; Ezk 36:27; Eph 1:19}} yet so as they come most freely, being made willing by His grace. {{proofs: Psa 110:3}}

10.2 This effectual call is of God's free and special grace alone, not from anything at all foreseen in man, nor from any power or agency in the creature, co-working with His special grace, {{proofs: 2Ti 1:9; Eph 2:8}} the creature being wholly passive therein, being dead in sins and trespasses, until being quickened and renewed by the Holy Spirit; {{proofs: 1Co 2:14; Eph 2:5; Jhn 5:25}} he is thereby enabled to answer this call, and to embrace the grace offered and conveyed in it, and that by no less power than that which raised up Christ from the dead. {{proofs: Eph 1:19-20}}

10.3 Elect infants dying in infancy are regenerated and saved by Christ through the Spirit; {{proofs: Jhn 3:3, 5-6}} who works when, and where, and how He pleases; {{proofs: Jhn 3:8}} so also are all other elect persons, who are incapable of being outwardly called by the ministry of the Word.

10.4 Others not elected, although they may be called by the ministry of the Word, and may have some common operations of the Spirit, {{proofs: Mat 22:14; 13:20-21; Heb 6:4-5}} yet not being effectually drawn by the Father, they neither will nor can truly come to Christ, and therefore cannot be saved, {{proofs: Jhn 6:44-45, 65; 1Jn 2:24-25}} much less can men that do not receive the Christian religion be saved: be they never so diligent to frame their lives according to the light of nature and the law of that religion they do profess. {{proofs: Act 4:12; Jhn 4:22; 17:3}}

units.title: Of Justification
content: 11.1 Those whom God effectually calls, he also freely justifies, {{proofs: Rom 3:24; 8:30}} not by infusing righteousness into them, but by pardoning their sins, and by accounting and accepting their persons as righteous; {{proofs: Rom 4:5-8; Eph 1:7}} not for anything wrought in them, or done by them, but for Christ's sake alone; {{proofs: 1Co 1:30-31; Rom 5:17-19}} not by imputing faith itself, the act of believing, or any other evangelical obedience to them, as their righteousness; but by imputing Christ's active obedience unto the whole law, and passive obedience in his death for their whole and sole righteousness, {{proofs: Php 3:8-9; Eph 2:8-10}} they receiving and resting on Him and His righteousness by faith, which faith they have not of themselves; it is the gift of God. {{proofs: Jhn 1:12; Rom 5:17}}

11.2 Faith thus receiving and resting on Christ and His righteousness, is the alone instrument of justification; {{proofs: Rom 3:28}} yet is not alone in the person justified, but is ever accompanied with all other saving graces, and is no dead faith, but works by love. {{proofs: Gal 5:6; Jas 2:17, 22, 26}}

11.3 Christ, by His obedience and death, did fully discharge the debt of all those who are justified; and did, by the sacrifice of Himself in the blood of his cross, undergoing in their stead the penalty due to them, make a proper, real, and full satisfaction to God's justice in their behalf; {{proofs: Heb 10:14; 1Pe 1:18-19; Isa 53:5-6}} yet, in as much as He was given by the Father for them, and His obedience and satisfaction accepted in their stead, and both freely, not for anything in them, {{proofs: Rom 8:32; 2Co 5:21}} their justification is only of free grace, that both the exact justice and rich grace of God might be glorified in the justification of sinners. {{proofs: Rom 3:26; Eph 1:6-7; 2:7}}

11.4 God did from all eternity decree to justify all the elect, {{proofs: Gal 3:8; 1Pe 1:2; 1Ti 2:6}} and Christ did in the fullness of time die for their sins, and rise again for their justification; {{proofs: Rom 4:25}} nevertheless, they are not justified personally, until the Holy Spirit in time does actually apply Christ to them. {{proofs: Col 1:21-22; Tit 3:4-7}}

11.5 God continues to forgive the sins of those that are justified, {{proofs: Mat 6:12; 1Jn 1:7, 9}} and although they can never fall from the state of justification, {{proofs: Jhn 10:28}} yet they may, by their sins, fall under God's fatherly displeasure; {{proofs: Psa 89:31-33}} and in that condition they usually do not have the light of his countenance restored to them, until they humble themselves, confess their sins, beg pardon, and renew their faith and repentance. {{proofs: Psa 32:5; Psa 51; Mat 26:75}}
11.6 The justification of believers under the Old Testament was, in all these respects, one and the same with the justification of believers under the New Testament. {{proofs: Gal 3:9; Rom 4:22-24}}

units.title: Of Adoption
content: 12.1 All those that are justified, God vouchsafed, in and for the sake of His only Son Jesus Christ, to make partakers of the grace of adoption, {{proofs: Eph 1:5; Gal 4:4-5}} by which they are taken into the number, and enjoy the liberties and privileges of the children of God, {{proofs: Jhn 1:12; Rom 8:17}} have His name put on them, {{proofs: 2Co 6:18; Rev 3:12}} receive the spirit of adoption, {{proofs: Rom 8:15}} have access to the throne of grace with boldness, are enabled to cry Abba, Father, {{proofs: Gal 4:6; Eph 2:18}} are pitied, {{proofs: Psa 103:13}} protected, {{proofs: Pro 14:26; 1Pe 5:7}} provided for, {{proofs: Heb 12:6}} and chastened by Him as by a Father, {{proofs: Isa 54:8-9}} yet never cast off, {{proofs: Lam 3:31}} but sealed to the day of redemption, {{proofs: Eph 4:30}} and inherit the promises as heirs of everlasting salvation. {{proofs: Heb 1:14; 6:12}}

units.title: Of Sanctification
content: 13.1 They who are united to Christ, effectually called, and regenerated, having a new heart and a new spirit created in them through the virtue of Christ's death and resurrection, are also farther sanctified, really and personally, {{proofs: Act 20:32; Rom 6:5-6}} through the same virtue, by his Word and Spirit dwelling in them; {{proofs: Jhn 17:17; Eph 3:16-19; 1Th 5:21-23}} the dominion of the whole body of sin is destroyed, {{proofs: Rom 6:14}} and the several lusts thereof are more and more weakened and mortified, {{proofs: Gal 5:24}} and they more and more quickened and strengthened in all saving graces, {{proofs: Col 1:11}} to the practice of all true holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord. {{proofs: 2Co 7:1; Heb 12:14}}

13.2 This sanctification is throughout, in the whole man, {{proofs: 1Th 5:23}} yet imperfect in this life; there abideth still some remnants of corruption in every part, {{proofs: Rom 7:18, 23}} whence ariseth a continual and irreconcilable war; the flesh lusting against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh. {{proofs: Gal 5:17; 1Pe 2:11}}

13.3 In which war, although the remaining corruption for a time may much prevail, {{proofs: Rom 7:23}} yet, through the continual supply of strength from the sanctifying Spirit of Christ, the regenerate part doth overcome; {{proofs: Rom 6:14}} and so the saints grow in grace, perfecting holiness in the fear of God, pressing after an heavenly life, in evangelical obedience to all the commands which Christ, as Head and King, in His Word hath prescribed to them. {{proofs: Eph 4:15-16; 2Co 3:18; 7:1}}

units.title: Of Saving Faith
content: 14.1 The grace of faith, whereby the elect are enabled to believe to the saving of their souls, is the work of the Spirit of Christ in their hearts, {{proofs: 2Co 4:13; Eph 2:8}} and is ordinarily wrought by the ministry of the Word; {{proofs: Rom 10:14, 17}} by which also, and by the administration of baptism and the Lord's supper, prayer, and other means appointed of God, it is increased and strengthened. {{proofs: Luk 17:5; 1Pe 2:2; Act 20:32}}

14.2 By this faith a Christian believeth to be true whatsoever is revealed in the Word for the authority of God himself, {{proofs: Act 24:14}} and also apprehendeth an excellency therein above all other writings and all things in the world, {{proofs: Psa 19:7-10}} as it bears forth the glory of God in His attributes, the excellency of Christ in His nature and offices, and the power and fullness of the Holy Spirit in His workings and operations: and so is enabled to cast his soul upon the truth thus believed; {{proofs: 2Ti 1:12}} and also acteth differently upon that which each particular passage thereof containeth; yielding obedience to the commands, {{proofs: Jhn 15:14}} trembling at the threatenings, {{proofs: Isa 116:2}} and embracing the promises of God for this life and that which is to come; {{proofs: Heb 11:13}} but the principle acts of saving faith have immediate relation to Christ, accepting, receiving, and resting upon Him alone for justification, sanctification, and eternal life, by virtue of the covenant of grace. {{proofs: Jhn 1:12; Act 16:31; Act 15:11}}

14.3 This faith, although it be in different degrees, and may be weak or strong, {{proofs: Heb 5:13-14; Mat 6:30; Rom 4:19-20}} yet it is in the least degree of it different in the kind or nature of it, as is all other saving grace, from the faith and common grace of temporary believers; {{proofs: 2Pe 1:1}} and therefore, though it may be many times assailed and weakened, yet it gets the victory, {{proofs: Eph 6:16; 1Jn 5:4-5}} growing up in many to the attainment of a full assurance through Christ, {{proofs: Heb 6:11-12; Col 2:2}} who is both the author and finisher of our faith. {{proofs: Heb 12:2}}

units.title: Of Repentance Unto Life And Salvation
content: 15.1 Such of the elect that are converted at riper years, having sometime lived in the state of nature, and therein served divers lusts and pleasures, God in their effectual calling giveth them repentance unto life. {{proofs: Tit 3:2-5}}

15.2 Whereas there is none that doth good and sinneth not, {{proofs: Ecc 7:20}} and the best of men may, through the power and deceitfulness of their corruption dwelling in them, with the prevalency of temptation, fall in to great sins and provocations; God hath, in the covenant of grace, mercifully provided that believers so sinning and falling be renewed through repentance unto salvation. {{proofs: Luk 22:31-32}}

15.3 This saving repentance is an evangelical grace, {{proofs: Zec 12:10; Act 11:18}} whereby a person, being by the Holy Spirit made sensible of the manifold evils of his sin, doth, by faith in Christ, humble himself for it with godly sorrow, detestation of it, and self-abhorrancy, {{proofs: Ezk 36:31; 2Co 7:11}} praying for pardon and strength of grace, with a purpose and endeavor, by supplies of the Spirit, to walk before God unto all well-pleasing in all things. {{proofs: Psa 119:6, 128}}

15.4 As repentance is to be continued through the whole course of our lives, upon the account of the body of death, and the motions thereof, so it is every man's duty to repent of his particular known sins particularly. {{proofs: Luk 19:8; 1Ti 1:13, 15}}

15.5 Such is the provision which God hath made through Christ in the covenant of grace for the preservation of believers unto salvation, that although there is no sin so small but it deserves damnation, {{proofs: Rom 6:23}} yet there is no sin so great that it shall bring damnation on them that repent, {{proofs: Isa 1:16-18; 55:7}} which makes the constant preaching of repentance necessary.

units.title: Of Good Works
content: 16.1 Good works are only such as God hath commanded in His Holy Word, {{proofs: Mic 6:8; Heb 13:21}} and not such as without the warrant thereof are devised by men out of blind zeal, or upon any pretense of good intentions. {{proofs: Mat 15:9; Isa 29:13}}

16.2 These good works, done in obedience to God's commandments, are the fruits and evidences of a true and lively faith; {{proofs: Jas 2:18, 22}} and by them believers manifest their thankfulness, {{proofs: Psa 116:12-13}} strengthen their assurance, {{proofs: 1Jn 2:3, 5; 2Pe 1:5-11}} edify their brethren, adorn the profession of the gospel, {{proofs: Mat 5:16}} stop the mouths of the adversaries, and glorify God, {{proofs: 1Ti 6:1; 1Pe 2:15; Php 1:11}} whose workmanship they are, created in Christ Jesus thereunto, {{proofs: Eph 2:10}} that having their fruit unto holiness they may have the end eternal life. {{proofs: Rom 6:22}}

16.3 Their ability to do good works is not all of themselves, but wholly from the Spirit of Christ; {{proofs: Jhn 15:4-5}} and that they may be enabled thereunto, besides the graces they have already received, there is necessary an actual influence of the same Holy Spirit, to work in them and to will and to do of His good pleasure; {{proofs: 2Co 3:5; Php 2:13}} yet they are not hereupon to grow negligent, as if they were not bound to perform any duty, unless upon a special motion of the Spirit, but they ought to be diligent in stirring up the grace of God that is in them. {{proofs: Php 2:12; Heb 6:11-12; Isa 64:7}}

16.4 They who in their obedience attain to the greatest height which is possible in this life, are so far from being able to supererogate, and to do more than God requires, as that they fall short of much which in duty they are bound to do. {{proofs: Job 9:2-3; Gal 5:17; Luk 17:10}}

16.5 We cannot by our best works merit pardon of sin or eternal life at the hand of God, by reason of the great disproportion that is between them and the glory to come, and the infinite distance that is between us and God, whom by them we can neither profit nor satisfy for the debt of our former sins; {{proofs: Rom 3:20; Eph 2:8-9; Rom 4:6}} but when we have done all we can, we have done but our duty, and are unprofitable servants; and because as they are good they proceed from His Spirit, {{proofs: Gal 5:22-23}} and as they are wrought by us they are defiled and mixed with so much weakness and imperfection, that they cannot endure the severity of God's judgement. {{proofs: Isa 64:6; Psa 43:2}}

16.6 Yet notwithstanding the persons of believers being accepted through Christ, their good works also are accepted in Him; {{proofs: Eph 1:5; 1Pe 1:5}} not as though they were in this life wholly unblamable and unreprovable in God's sight, but that He, looking upon them in His Son, is pleased to accept and reward that which is sincere, although accompanied with many weaknesses and imperfection. {{proofs: Mat 25:21, 23; Heb 6:10}}

16.7 Works done by unregenerate men, although for the matter of them they may things which God commands, and of good use both to themselves and to others; {{proofs: 2Ki 10:30; 1Ki 21:27, 29}} yet because they proceed not from a heart purified by faith, {{proofs: Gen 4:5; Heb 11:4, 6}} nor are done in a right manner according to the Word, {{proofs: 1Co 13:1}} nor to a right end, the glory of God, {{proofs: Mat 6:2, 5}} they are therefore sinful, and cannot please God, nor make a man meet to receive the grace from God, {{proofs: Amo 5:21-22; Rom 9:16; Tit 3:5}} and yet their neglect for them is more sinful and displeasing to God. {{proofs: Job 21:14-15; Mat 25:41-43}}

units.title: Of The Perseverance Of The Saints
content: 17.1 Those whom God has accepted in the beloved, effectually called and sanctified by His Spirit, and given the precious faith of His elect unto, can neither totally nor finally fall from the state of grace, but shall certainly persevere therein to the end, and be eternally saved, seeing the gifts and callings of God are without repentance, from which source He still begets and nourisheseth in them faith, repentance, love, joy, hope, and all the graces of the Spirit unto immortality; {{proofs: Jhn 10:28-29; Php 1:6; 2Ti 2:19; 1Jn 2:19}} and though many storms and floods arise and beat against them, yet they shall never be able to take them off that foundation and rock which by faith they are fastened upon; notwithstanding, through unbelief and the temptations of Satan, the sensible sight of the light and love of God may for a time be clouded and obscured from them, {{proofs: Psa 89:31-32; 1Co 11:32}} yet He is still the same, and they shall be sure to be kept by the power of God unto salvation, where they shall enjoy their purchased possession, they being engraved upon the palm of His hands, and their names having been written in the book of life from all eternity. {{proofs: Mal 3:6}}

17.2 This perseverance of the saints depends not upon their own free will, but upon the immutability of the decree of election, {{proofs: Rom 8:30; 9:11, 16}} flowing from the free and unchangeable love of God the Father, upon the efficacy of the merit and intercession of Jesus Christ and union with Him, {{proofs: Rom 5:9-10; Jhn 14:19}} the oath of God, {{proofs: Heb 6:17-18}} the abiding of his Spirit, and the seed of God within them, {{proofs: 1Jn 3:9}} and the nature of the covenant of grace; {{proofs: Jer 32:40}} from all which ariseth also the certainty and infallibility thereof.

17.3 And though they may, through the temptation of Satan and of the world, the prevalency of corruption remaining in them, and the neglect of means of their preservation, fall into grievous sins, and for a time continue therein, {{proofs: Mat 26:70, 72, 74}} whereby they incur God's displeasure and grieve His Holy Spirit, {{proofs: Isa 64:5, 9; Eph 4:30}} come to have their graces and comforts impaired, {{proofs: Psa 51:10, 12}} have their hearts hardened, and their consciences wounded, {{proofs: Psa 32:3-4}} hurt and scandalize others, and bring temporal judgments upon themselves, {{proofs: 2Sa 12:14}} yet shall they renew their repentance and be preserved through faith in Christ Jesus to the end. {{proofs: Luk 22:32, 61-62}}

units.title: Of The Assurance Of Grace And Salvation
content: 18.1 Although temporary believers and other unregenerate men, may vainly deceive themselves with false hopes and carnal presumptions of being in the favor of God and in a state of salvation, which hope of theirs shall perish; {{proofs: Job 8:13-14; Mat 7:22-23}} yet such as truly believe in the Lord Jesus, and love Him in sincerity, endeavouring to walk in all good conscience before Him, may in this life be certainly assured that they are in the state of grace, and may rejoice in the hope of the glory of God, {{proofs: 1Jn 2:3; 3:14, 18-19, 21, 24; 5:13}} which hope shall never make them ashamed. {{proofs: Rom 5:2, 5}}

18.2 This certainty is not a bare conjectural and probable persuasion grounded upon a fallible hope, but an infallible assurance of faith, {{proofs: Heb 6:11, 19}} founded on the blood and righteousness of Christ revealed in the Gospel; {{proofs: Heb 6:17-18}} and also upon the inward evidence of those graces of the Spirit unto which promises are made, {{proofs: 2Pe 1:4-5, 10-11}} and on the testimony of the Spirit of adoption, witnessing with our spirits that we are the children of God; {{proofs: Rom 8:15-16}} and, as a fruit thereof, keeping the heart both humble and holy. {{proofs: 1Jn 3:1-3}}

18.3 This infallible assurance does not so belong to the essence of faith, but that a true believer may wait long, and conflict with many difficulties before he be partaker of it; {{proofs: Isa 50:10; Psa 88; 77:1-12}} yet being enabled by the Spirit to know the things which are freely given him of God, he may, without extraordinary revelation, in the right use of means, attain thereunto: {{proofs: 1Jn 4:13; Heb 6:11-12}} and therefore it is the duty of every one to give all diligence to make his calling and election sure, that thereby his heart may be enlarged in peace and joy in the Holy Spirit, in love and thankfulness to God, and in strength and cheerfulness in the duties of obedience, the proper fruits of this assurance; {{proofs: Rom 5:1-2, 5; 14:17; Psa 119:32}} ⏤so far is it from inclining men to looseness. {{proofs: Rom 6:1-2; Tit 2:11-12, 14}}

18.4 True believers may have the assurance of their salvation divers ways shaken, diminished, and intermitted; as by negligence in preserving of it, {{proofs: Sng 5:2-3, 6}} by falling into some special sin which woundeth the conscience and grieveth the Spirit; {{proofs: Psa 51:8, 12, 14}} by some sudden or vehement temptation, {{proofs: Psa 116:11; 77:7-8; 31:22}} by God's withdrawing the light of His countenance, and suffering even such as fear Him to walk in darkness and to have no light, {{proofs: Psa 30:7}} yet are they never destitute of the seed of God {{proofs: 1Jn 3:9}} and life of faith, {{proofs: Luk 22:32}} that love of Christ and the brethren, that sincerity of heart and conscience of duty out of which, by the operation of the Spirit, this assurance may in due time be revived, {{proofs: Psa 42:5, 11}} and by the which, in the meantime, they are preserved from utter despair. {{proofs: Lam 3:26-31}}

units.title: Of The Law Of God
content: 19.1 God gave to Adam a law of universal obedience written in his heart, and a particular precept of not eating the fruit of the tree of knowledge of good and evil; {{proofs: Gen 1:27; Ecc 7:29}} by which He bound him and all his posterity to personal, entire, exact, and perpetual obedience; {{proofs: Rom 10:5}} promised life upon the fulfilling, and threatened death upon the breach of it, and endued him with power and ability to keep it. {{proofs: Gal 3:10, 12}}

19.2 The same law that was first written in the heart of man continued to be a perfect rule of righteousness after the fall, {{proofs: Rom 2:14-15}} and was delivered by God upon Mount Sinai, in ten commandments, and written in two tables, the four first containing our duty towards God, and the other six, our duty to man. {{proofs: Deu 10:4}}

19.3 Besides this law, commonly called moral, God was pleased to give to the people of Israel ceremonial laws, containing several typical ordinances, partly of worship, prefiguring Christ, His graces, actions, sufferings, and benefits; {{proofs: Heb 10:1; Col 2:17}} and partly holding forth divers instructions of moral duties, {{proofs: 1Co 5:7}} all which ceremonial laws being appointed only to the time of reformation, are, by Jesus Christ the true Messiah and only law-giver, who was furnished with power from the Father for that end abrogated and taken away. {{proofs: Col 2:14, 16-17; Eph 2:14, 16}}

19.4 To them also He gave sundry judicial laws, which expired together with the state of that people, not obliging any now by virtue of that institution; their general equity only being of modern use. {{proofs: 1Co 9:8-10}}

19.5 The moral law does for ever bind all, as well justified persons as others, to the obedience thereof, {{proofs: Rom 13:8-10; Jas 2:8, 10-12}} and that not only in regard of the matter contained in it, but also in respect of the authority of God the Creator, who gave it; {{proofs: Jas 2:10-11}} neither doth Christ in the Gospel any way dissolve, but much strengthen this obligation. {{proofs: Mat 5:17-19; Rom 3:31}}

19.6 Although true believers are not under the law as a covenant of works, to be thereby justified or condemned, {{proofs: Rom 6:14; Gal 2:16; Rom 8:1; 10:4}} yet it is of great use to them as well as to others, in that as a rule of life, informing them of the will of God and their duty, it directs and binds them to walk accordingly; discovering also the sinful pollutions of their natures, hearts, and lives, so as examining themselves thereby, they may come to further conviction of, humiliation for, and hatred against, sin; {{proofs: Rom 3:20; 7:7}} together with a clearer sight of the need they have of Christ and the perfection of His obedience; it is likewise of use to the regenerate to restrain their corruptions, in that it forbids sin; and the threatenings of it serve to show what even their sins deserve, and what afflictions in this life they may expect for them, although freed from the curse and unallayed rigour thereof. The promises of it likewise show them God's approbation of obedience, and what blessings they may expect upon the performance thereof, though not as due to them by the law as a covenant of works; so as man's doing good and refraining from evil, for the law encourageth to the one and deterreth from the other, is no evidence of his being under the law and not under grace. {{proofs: Rom 6:12-14; 1Pe 3:8-13}}

19.7 Neither are the aforementioned uses of the law contrary to the grace of the Gospel, but do sweetly comply with it, {{proofs: Gal 3:21}} the Spirit of Christ subduing and enabling the will of man to do that freely and cheerfully which the will of God, revealed in the law, requireth to be done. {{proofs: Ezk 36:27}}

units.title: Of The Gospel And Of The Extent Of The Grace Thereof
content: 20.1 The covenant of works being broken by sin, and made unprofitable unto life, God was pleased to give forth the promise of Christ, the seed of the woman, as the means of calling the elect, and begetting in them faith and repentance; {{proofs: Gen 3:15}} in this promise the gospel, as to the substance of it, was revealed, and [is] therein effectual for the conversion and salvation of sinners. {{proofs: Rev 13:8}}

20.2 This promise of Christ, and salvation by Him, is revealed only by the Word of God; {{proofs: Rom 1}} neither do the works of creation or providence, with the light of nature, make discovery of Christ, or of grace by Him, so much as in a general or obscure way; {{proofs: Rom 10:14-15, 17}} much less that men destitute of the revelation of Him by the promise or Gospel, should be enabled thereby to attain saving faith or repentance. {{proofs: Pro 29:18; Isa 25:7; 60:2-3}}

20.3 The revelation of the gospel unto sinners, made in divers times and by sundry parts, with the addition of promises and precepts for the obedience required therein, as to the nations and persons to whom it is granted, is merely of the sovereign will and good pleasure of God; {{proofs: Psa 147:20; Act 16:7}} not being annexed by virtue of any promise to the due improvement of men's natural abilities, by virtue of common light received without it, which none everdid make, or can do so; {{proofs: Rom 1:18-32}} and therefore in all ages, the preaching of the Gospel has been granted unto persons and nations, as to the extent or straitening of it, in great variety, according to the counsel of the will of God.

20.4 Although the Gospel be the only outward means of revealing Christ and saving grace, and is, as such, abundantly sufficient thereunto; yet that men who are dead in trespasses may be born again, quickened or regenerated, there is moreover necessary an effectual, insuperable work of the Holy Spirit upon the whole soul, for the producing in them a new spiritual life; {{proofs: Psa 110:3; 1Co 2:14; Eph 1:19-20}} without which no other means will effect their conversion unto God. {{proofs: Jhn 6:44; 2Co 4:4, 6}}

units.title: Of Christian Liberty And Liberty Of Conscience
content: 21.1 The liberty which Christ has purchased for believers under the Gospel, consists in their freedom from the guilt of sin, the condemning wrath of God, the severity and curse of the law, {{proofs: Gal 3:13}} and in their being delivered from this present evil world, {{proofs: Gal 1:4}} bondage to Satan, {{proofs: Act 26:18}} and dominion of sin, {{proofs: Rom 8:3}} from the evil of afflictions, {{proofs: Rom 8:28}} the fear and sting of death, the victory of the grave, {{proofs: 1Co 15:54-57}} and everlasting damnation: {{proofs: 2Th 1:10}} as also in their free access to God, and their yielding obedience unto Him, not out of slavish fear, {{proofs: Rom 8:15}} but a child-like love and willing mind. {{proofs: Luk 1:73-75; 1Jn 4:18}} All which were common also to believers under the law for the substance of them; {{proofs: Gal 3}} but under the New Testament the liberty of Christians is further enlarged, in their freedom from the yoke of the ceremonial law, to which the Jewish church was subjected, and in greater boldness of access to the throne of grace, and in fuller communications of the free Spirit of God, than believers under the law did ordinarily partake of. {{proofs: Jhn 7:38-39; Heb 10:19-21}}

21.2 God alone is Lord of the conscience, {{proofs: Jas 4:12; Rom 14:4}} and hath left it free from the doctrines and commandments of men which are in any thing contrary to His Word, or not contained in it. {{proofs: Act 4:19, 29; 1Co 7:23; Mat 15:9}} So that to believe such doctrines, or obey such commands out of conscience, is to betray true liberty of conscience; {{proofs: Col 2:20, 22-23}} and the requiring of an implicit faith, and absolute and blind obedience, is to destroy liberty of conscience and reason also. {{proofs: 1Co 3:5; 2Co 1:24}}

21.3 They who upon pretence of Christian liberty do practice any sin, or cherish any sinful lust, as they do thereby pervert the main design of the grace of the gospel to their own destruction, {{proofs: Rom 6:1-2}} so they wholly destroy the end of Christian liberty, which is, that being delivered out of the hands of all our enemies, we might serve the Lord without fear, in holiness and righteousness before Him, all the days of our lives. {{proofs: Gal 5:13; 2Pe 2:18, 21}}

units.title: Of Religious Worship And The Sabbath Day
content: 22.1 The light of nature shows that there is a God, who has lordship and sovereignty over all; is just, good and doth good to all; and is therefore to be feared, loved, praised, called upon, trusted in, and served, with all the heart and all the soul, and with all the might. {{proofs: Jer 10:7; Mrk 12:33}} But the acceptable way of worshipping the true God, is instituted by Himself, {{proofs: Deu 12:32}} and so limited by his own revealed will, that He may not be worshipped according to the imagination and devices of men, nor the suggestions of Satan, under any visible representations, or any other way not prescribed in the Holy Scriptures. {{proofs: Exo 20:4-6}}

22.2 Religious worship is to be given to God the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit, and to Him alone; {{proofs: Mat 4:9-10; Jhn 6:23; Mat 28:19}} not to angels, saints, or any other creatures; {{proofs: Rom 1:25; Col 2:18; Rev 19:10}} and since the fall, not without a mediator, {{proofs: Jhn 14:6}} nor in the mediation of any other but Christ alone. {{proofs: 1Ti 2:5}}

22.3 Prayer, with thanksgiving, being one part of natural worship, is by God required of all men. {{proofs: Psa 95:1-7; 65:2}} But that it may be accepted, it is to be made in the name of the Son, {{proofs: Jhn 14:13-14}} by the help of the Spirit, {{proofs: Rom 8:26}} according to His will; {{proofs: 1Jn 5:14}} with understanding, reverence, humility, fervency, faith, love, and perseverance; and when with others, in a known tongue. {{proofs: 1Co 14:16-17}}

22.4 Prayer is to be made for things lawful, and for all sorts of men living, or that shall live hereafter; {{proofs: 1Ti 2:1-2; 2Sa 7:29}} but not for the dead, {{proofs: 2Sa 12:21-23}} nor for those of whom it may be known that they have sinned the sin unto death. {{proofs: 1Jn 5:16}}

22.5 The reading of the Scriptures, {{proofs: 1Ti 4:13}} preaching, and hearing the Word of God, {{proofs: 2Ti 4:2; Luk 8:18}} teaching and admonishing one another in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, singing with grace in our hearts to the Lord; {{proofs: Col 3:16; Eph 5:19}} as also the administration of baptism, {{proofs: Mat 28:19-20}} and the Lord's supper, {{proofs: 1Co 11:26}} are all parts of religious worship of God, to be performed in obedience to Him, with understanding, faith, reverence, and godly fear; moreover, solemn humiliation, with fastings, {{proofs: Est 4:16; Jol 2:12}} and thanksgivings, upon special occasions, ought to be used in an holy and religious manner. {{proofs: Exo 15:1-19; Psa 107}}

22.6 Neither prayer nor any other part of religious worship, is now under the Gospel, tied unto, or made more acceptable by any place in which it is performed, or towards which it is directed; but God is to be worshipped everywhere in spirit and in truth; {{proofs: Jhn 4:21; Mal 1:11; 1Ti 2:8}} as in private families {{proofs: Act 10:2}} daily, {{proofs: Mat 6:11; Psa 55:17}} and in secret each one by himself; {{proofs: Mat 6:6}} so more solemnly in the public assemblies, which are not carelessly nor wilfully to be neglected or forsaken, when God by His word or providence calls thereunto. {{proofs: Heb 10:25; Act 2:42}}

22.7 As it is the law of nature, that in general a proportion of time, by God's appointment, be set apart for the worship of God, so by His Word, in a positive moral, and perpetual commandment, binding all men, in all ages, He has particularly appointed one day in seven for a sabbath to be kept holy unto Him, {{proofs: Exo 20:8}} which from the beginning of the world to the resurrection of Christ was the last day of the week, and from the resurrection of Christ was changed into the first day of the week, which is called the Lord's Day: {{proofs: 1Co 16:1-2; Act 20:7; Rev 1:10}} and is to be continued to the end of the world as the Christian Sabbath, the observation of the last day of the week being abolished.

22.8 The sabbath is then kept holy unto the Lord, when men, after a due preparing of their hearts, and ordering their common affairs aforehand, do not only observe a holy rest all day, from their own works, words and thoughts, about their worldly employment and recreations, {{proofs: Isa 58:13; Neh 13:15-22}} but are also taken up the whole time in the public and private exercises of his worship, and in the duties of necessity and mercy. {{proofs: Mat 12:1-13}}

units.title: Of Lawful Oaths And Vows
content: 23.1 A lawful oath is a part of religious worship, wherein the person swearing in truth, righteousness, and judgment, solemnly calls God to witness what he swears, {{proofs: Exo 20:7; Deu 10:20; Jer 4:2}} and to judge him according to the truth or falseness thereof. {{proofs: 2Ch 6:22-23}}

23.2 The name of God only is that by which men ought to swear; and therein it is to be used, with all holy fear and reverence; therefore to swear vainly or rashly by that glorious and dreadful name, or to swear at all by any other thing, is sinful, and to be abhorred; {{proofs: Mat 5:34, 37; Jas 5:12}} yet as in matter of weight and moment, for confirmation of truth, and ending all strife, an oath is warranted by the Word of God; {{proofs: Heb 6:16; 2Co 1:23}} so a lawful oath being imposed by lawful authority in such matters, ought to be taken. {{proofs: Neh 13:25}}

23.3 Whosoever taketh an oath warranted by the Word of God, ought duly to consider the weightiness of so solemn an act, and therein to avouch nothing but what he knoweth to be truth; for that by rash, false, and vain oaths, the Lord is provoked, and for them this land mourns. {{proofs: Lev 19:12; Jer 23:10}}
23.4 An oath is to be taken in the plain and common sense of the words, without equivocation or mental reservation. {{proofs: Psa 24:4}}

23.5 A vow, which is not to be made to any creature, but to God alone, is to be made and performed with all religious care and faithfulness; {{proofs: Psa 76:11; Gen 28:20-22}} but popish monastical vows of perpetual single life, {{proofs: 1Co 7:2, 9}} professed poverty, {{proofs: Eph 4:28}} and regular obedience, are so far from being degrees of higher perfection, that they are superstitious and sinful snares, in which no Christian may entangle himself. {{proofs: Mat 19:1}}

units.title: Of The Civil Magistrate
content: 24.1 God, the supreme Lord and King of all the world, hath ordained civil magistrates to be under Him, over the people, for His own glory and the public good; and to this end has armed them with the power of the sword, for defence and encouragement of them that do good, and for the punishment of evil doers. {{proofs: Rom 13:1-4}}

24.2 It is lawful for Christians to accept and execute the office of a magistrate when called thereunto; in the management whereof, as they ought especially to maintain justice and peace, {{proofs: 2Sa 23:3; Psa 82:3-4}} according to the wholesome laws of each kingdom and commonwealth, so for that end they may lawfully now, under the New Testament, wage war upon just and necessary occasions. {{proofs: Luk 3:14}}

24.3 Civil magistrates being set up by God for the ends aforesaid; subjection, in all lawful things commanded by them, ought to be yielded by us in the Lord, not only for wrath, but for conscience' sake; {{proofs: Rom 13:5-7; 1Pe 2:17}} and we ought to make supplications and prayers for kings and all that are in authority, that under them we may live a quiet and peaceable life, in all godliness and honesty. {{proofs: 1Ti 2:1-2}}

units.title: Of Marriage
content: 25.1 Marriage is to be between one man and one woman; neither is it lawful for any man to have more than one wife, nor for any woman to have more than one husband at the same time. {{proofs: Gen 2:24; Mal 2:15; Mat 19:5-6}}

25.2 Marriage was ordained for the mutual help of husband and wife, {{proofs: Gen 2:18}} for the increase of mankind with a legitimate issue, {{proofs: Gen 1:28}} and the preventing of uncleanness. {{proofs: 1Co 7:2, 9}}

25.3 It is lawful for all sorts of people to marry, who are able with judgment to give their consent; {{proofs: Heb 13:4; 1Ti 4:3}} yet it is the duty of Christians to marry in the Lord; {{proofs: 1Co 7:39}} and therefore such as profess the true religion, should not marry with infidels, or idolaters; neither should such as are godly, be unequally yoked, by marrying with such as are wicked in their life, or maintain damnable heresy. {{proofs: Neh 13:25-27}}

25.4 Marriage ought not to be within the degrees of consanguinity or affinity, forbidden in the Word; {{proofs: Lev 18}} nor can such incestuous marriages ever be made lawful, by any law of man or consent of parties, so as those persons may live together as man and wife. {{proofs: Mrk 6:18; 1Co 5:1}}

units.title: Of The Church
content: 26.1 The catholic or universal church, which (with respect to the internal work of the Spirit and truth of grace) may be called invisible, consists of the whole number of the elect, that have been, are, or shall be gathered into one, under Christ, the head thereof; and is the spouse, the body, the fulness of him that fills all in all. {{proofs: Heb 12:23; Col 1:18; Eph 1:10, 22-23; 5:23, 27, 32}}

26.2 All persons throughout the world, professing the faith of the gospel, and obedience unto God by Christ according unto it, not destroying their own profession by any errors everting the foundation, or unholiness of conversation, are and may be called visible saints; {{proofs: 1Co 1:2; Act 11:26}} and of such ought all particular congregations to be constituted. {{proofs: Rom 1:7; Eph 1:20-22}}

26.3 The purest churches under heaven are subject to mixture and error; {{proofs: 1Co 5; Rev 2-3}} and some have so degenerated as to become no churches of Christ, but synagogues of Satan; {{proofs: Rev 18:2; 2Th 2:11-12}} nevertheless Christ always hath had, and ever shall have a kingdom in this world, to the end thereof, of such as believe in Him, and make profession of His name. {{proofs: Mat 16:18; Psa 72:17; 102:28; Rev 12:17}}

26.4 The Lord Jesus Christ is the Head of the church, in whom, by the appointment of the Father, all power for the calling, institution, order or government of the church, is invested in a supreme and sovereign manner; {{proofs: Col 1:18; Mat 28:18-20; Eph 4:11-12}} neither can the Pope of Rome in any sense be head thereof, but is that antichrist, that man of sin, and son of perdition, that exalts himself in the church against Christ, and all that is called God; whom the Lord shall destroy with the brightness of his coming. {{proofs: 2Th 2:2-9}}

26.5 In the execution of this power wherewith He is so intrusted, the Lord Jesus calls out of the world unto Himself, through the ministry of His Word, by His Spirit, those that are given unto him by His Father, {{proofs: Jhn 10:16; 12:32}} that they may walk before Him in all the ways of obedience, which he prescribes to them in His Word. {{proofs: Mat 28:20}} Those thus called, He commandeth to walk together in particular societies, or churches, for their mutual edification, and the due performance of that public worship, which He requireth of them in the world. {{proofs: Mat 18:15-20}}

26.6 The members of these churches are saints by calling, visibly manifesting and evidencing (in and by their profession and walking) their obedience unto that call of Christ; {{proofs: Rom 1:7; 1Co 1:2}} and do willingly consent to walk together, according to the appointment of Christ; giving up themselves to the Lord, and one to another, by the will of God, in professed subjection to the ordinances of the Gospel. {{proofs: Act 2:41-42; 5:13-14; 2Co 9:13}}

26.7 To each of these churches therefore gathered, according to His mind declared in His Word, He has given all that power and authority, which is in any way needful for their carrying on that order in worship and discipline, which He hath instituted for them to observe; with commands and rules for the due and right exerting, and executing of that power. {{proofs: Mat 18:17-18; 1Co 5:4-5, 13; 2Co 2:6-8}}

26.8 A particular church, gathered and completely organized according to the mind of Christ, consists of officers and members; and the officers appointed by Christ to be chosen and set apart by the church (so called and gathered), for the peculiar administration of ordinances, and execution of power or duty, which He intrusts them with, or calls them to, to be continued to the end of the world, are bishops or elders, and deacons. {{proofs: Act 20:17, 28; Php 1:1}}

26.9 The way appointed by Christ for the calling of any person, fitted and gifted by the Holy Spirit, unto the office of bishop or elder in a church, is, that he be chosen thereunto by the common suffrage of the church itself; {{proofs: Act 14:23}} and solemnly set apart by fasting and prayer, with imposition of hands of the eldership of the church, if there be any before constituted therein; {{proofs: 1Ti 4:14}} and of a deacon that he be chosen by the like suffrage, and set apart by prayer, and the like imposition of hands. {{proofs: Act 6:3, 5-6}}

26.10 The work of pastors being constantly to attend the service of Christ, in His churches, in the ministry of the word and prayer, with watching for their souls, as they that must give an account to Him; {{proofs: Act 6:4; Heb 13:17}} it is incumbent on the churches to whom they minister, not only to give them all due respect, but also to communicate to them of all their good things according to their ability, {{proofs: 1Ti 5:17-18; Gal 6:6-7}} so as they may have a comfortable supply, without being themselves entangled in secular affairs; {{proofs: 2Ti 2:4}} and may also be capable of exercising hospitality towards others; {{proofs: 1Ti 3:2}} and this is required by the law of nature, and by the express order of our Lord Jesus, who has ordained that they that preach the Gospel should live of the Gospel. {{proofs: 1Co 9:6-14}}

26.11 Although it be incumbent on the bishops or pastors of the churches, to be instant in preaching the Word, by way of office, yet the work of preaching the word is not so peculiarly confined to them but that others also gifted and fitted by the Holy Spirit for it, and approved and called by the church, may and ought to perform it. {{proofs: Act 11:19-21; 1Pe 4:10-11}}

26.12 As all believers are bound to join themselves to particular churches, when and where they have opportunity so to do; so all that are admitted unto the privileges of a church, are also under the censures and government thereof, according to the rule of Christ. {{proofs: 1Th 5:14; 2Th 3:6, 14-15}}

26.13 No church members, upon any offence taken by them, having performed their duty required of them towards the person they are offended at, ought to disturb any church-order, or absent themselves from the assemblies of the church, or administration of any ordinances, upon the account of such offence at any of their fellow members, but to wait upon Christ, in the further proceeding of the church. {{proofs: Mat 18:15-17; Eph 4:2-3}}

26.14 As each church, and all the members of it, are bound to pray continually for the good and prosperity of all the churches of Christ, {{proofs: Eph 6:18; Psa 122:6}} in all places, and upon all occasions to further every it (one within the bounds of their places and callings, in the exercise of their gifts and graces) so the churches, when planted by the providence of God, so as they may enjoy opportunity and advantage for it, ought to hold communion among themselves, for their peace, increase of love, and mutual edification. {{proofs: Rom 16:1-2; 3Jn 8-10}}

26.15 In cases of difficulties or differences, either in point of doctrine or administration, wherein either the churches in general are concerned, or any one church, in their peace, union, and edification; or any member or members of any church are injured, in or by any proceedings in censures not agreeable to truth and order: it is according to the mind of Christ, that many churches holding communion together, do, by their messengers, meet to consider, and give their advice in or about that matter in difference, to be reported to all the churches concerned; {{proofs: Act 15:2, 4, 6, 22-23, 25}} howbeit these messengers assembled, are not intrusted with any church-power properly so called; or with any jurisdiction over the churches themselves, to exercise any censures either over any churches or persons; or to impose their determination on the churches or officers. {{proofs: 2Co 1:24; 1Jn 4:1}}

units.title: Of The Communion Of The Saints
content: 27.1 All saints that are united to Jesus Christ, their Head, by His Spirit, and faith, although they are not made thereby one person with Him, have fellowship in His graces, sufferings, death, resurrection, and glory; {{proofs: 1Jn 1:3; Jhn 1:16; Php 3:10; Rom 6:5-6}} and, being united to one another in love, they have communion in each others gifts and graces, {{proofs: Eph 4:15-16; 1Co 12:7; 3:21-23}} and are obliged to the performance of such duties, public and private, in an orderly way, as do conduce to their mutual good, both in the inward and outward man. {{proofs: 1Th 5:11, 14; Rom 1:12; 1Jn 3:17-18; Gal 6:10}}

27.2 Saints by profession are bound to maintain a holy fellowship and communion in the worship of God, and in performing such other spiritual services as tend to their mutual edification; {{proofs: Heb 10:24-25; 3:12-13}} as also in relieving each other in outward things according to their several abilities, and necessities; {{proofs: Act 11:29-30}} which communion, according to the rule of the gospel, though especially to be exercised by them, in the relation wherein they stand, whether in families, {{proofs: Eph 6:4}} or churches, {{proofs: 1Co 12:14-27}} yet, as God offereth opportunity, is to be extended to all the household of faith, even all those who in every place call upon the name of the Lord Jesus; nevertheless their communion one with another as saints, does not take away or infringe the title or propriety which each man has in his goods and possessions. {{proofs: Act 5:4; Eph 4:28}}

units.title: Of Baptism And The Lord's Supper
content: 28.1 Baptism and the Lord's Supper are ordinances of positive and sovereign institution, appointed by the Lord Jesus, the only Lawgiver, to be continued in His church to the end of the world. {{proofs: Mat 28:19-20; 1Co 11:26}}
28.2 These holy appointments are to be administered by those only who are qualified and thereunto called, according to the commission of Christ. {{proofs: Mat 28:19; 1Co 4:1}}

units.title: Of Baptism
content: 29.1 Baptism is an ordinance of the New Testament, ordained by Jesus Christ, to be unto the party baptized, a sign of his fellowship with Him, in His death and resurrection; of his being engrafted into Him; {{proofs: Rom 6:3-5; Col 2:12; Gal 3:27}} of remission of sins; {{proofs: Mrk 1:4; Act 22:16}} and of giving up unto God, through Jesus Christ, to live and walk in newness of life. {{proofs: Rom 6:4}}

29.2 Those who do actually profess repentance towards God, faith in, and obedience to, our Lord Jesus Christ, are the only proper subjects of this ordinance. {{proofs: Mrk 16:16; Act 8:36-37; Act 2:41; Act 8:12; Act 18:8}}

29.3 The outward element to be used in this ordinance is water, wherein the party is to be baptized, in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit. {{proofs: Mat 28:19-20; Act 8:38}}
29.4 Immersion, or dipping of the person in water, is necessary to the due administration of this ordinance. {{proofs: Mat 3:16; Jhn 3:23}}

units.title: Of The Lord's Supper
content: 30.1 The Supper of the Lord Jesus was instituted by Him the same night wherein He was betrayed, to be observed in His churches, unto the end of the world, for the perpetual remembrance, and showing to all the world the sacrifice of Himself in His death, {{proofs: 1Co 11:23-26}} confirmation of the faith of believers in all the benefits thereof, their spiritual nourishment, and growth in Him, their further engagement in, and to all duties which they owe to Him; and to be a bond and pledge of their communion with Him, and with each other. {{proofs: 1Co 10:16-17, 21}}
